coredata.dc:title: Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 encapsulating gold nanoclusters and carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescent detection of adenosine triphosphate and cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wei
Jiang, Xue
Wu, Yanhua
Jiang, Jing
Liu, Xin
Liu, Yibing
Wang, Wei
Lai, Jinyu
Wang, Xinghua
coredata.dc:description: A novel nanoprobe was prepared by encapsulating carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) for sensitive detecting adenosine triphosphate (ATP). Under excitation at 360 nm, the obtained CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe exhibits dual-emissions at 469 nm and 660 nm, respectively, corresponding to the fluorescence emission of CDs and the aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) of AuNCs. The framework of ZIF-8 in this probe can be degraded by ATP due to the coordination competition of ATP and 2-Methylimidazole towards zinc ion (Zn2+), resulting in the release of CDs and AuNCs. The following dispersion of CDs would improve efficiencies of the fluorescence excitation and the consequent emission of CDs. On the contrary, the AIEE of AuNCs would be decreased spontaneously after the AuNCs originally restricted in ZIF-8 were allowed to escape. The intensity ratio of fluorescence at 469 nm to that at 660 nm (I 469/I 660) was conveniently employed as the response signal for representing the amount of ATP. This nanoprobe exhibits excellent sensitivity and selectivity toward ATP, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.061 μM. Besides, low cytotoxicity of this nanoprobe facilitates its application as a fluorescent indicator in fluorescence imaging of living cells. Encapsulating two types of fluorescent nanomaterials by a degradable ZIF-8 structure makes the ratiometric fluorescence response of the nanocomposite probe towards the target analyte that destroys the ZIF-8 structure possible, and simplifies the application of the probe.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorometry
Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8
Gold nanoclusters
Carbon dots
Adenosine triphosphate
Cellular imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images with size distributions of CDs (A) and AuNCs (B). SEM images of CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 in absence (C) and presence (D) of ATP (100 μM).
Full scan survey XPS spectrum of AuNCs (A). XPS spectrum of Au in AuNCs (B). Full scan survey XPS spectrum of CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 (C). XPS spectrum of Au in CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 (D).
TEM image (left) and EDX elemental mapping results (right) of CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8.

Fluorescence emission spectra (A–B) and decay curves of CDs (C–D), AuNCs, ZIF-8 and their derivative nanocomposites. Excitation wavelength was 360 nm. Emission wavelengths for the decay profiles illustrated in C and D were 469 nm and 660 nm, respectively. Concentration of ATP was 300 μM.

Fluorescence spectra of the sensing system with ATP at various initial concentrations (A) (0.2, 10, 30, 50, 80, 100, 200, 300 μM). Linear relationship between response signals and ATP concentrations (B).

Confocal fluorescence images of HepG2 cell groups treated with different incubation procedures for 2 h (a, 37 °C; b, 4 °C; c, 37 °C in presence of 10 mM Ca2+). Excitation wavelength, 360 nm. Wavelength ranges, 400–550 nm for blue channel, 550–700 nm for red channel. Amount of CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8, 50 μL. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram of ATP assay using CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 as a fluorescent probe.
Results of detecting ATP in real human fluid samples by the present nanoprobe (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 encapsulating gold nanoclusters and carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescent detection of adenosine triphosphate and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First Hospital of Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Division of Clinical Research, The First Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel nanoprobe was prepared by encapsulating carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) for sensitive detecting adenosine triphosphate (ATP). Under excitation at 360 nm, the obtained CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe exhibits dual-emissions at 469 nm and 660 nm, respectively, corresponding to the fluorescence emission of CDs and the aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) of AuNCs. The framework of ZIF-8 in this probe can be degraded by ATP due to the coordination competition of ATP and 2-Methylimidazole towards zinc ion (Zn2+), resulting in the release of CDs and AuNCs. The following dispersion of CDs would improve efficiencies of the fluorescence excitation and the consequent emission of CDs. On the contrary, the AIEE of AuNCs would be decreased spontaneously after the AuNCs originally restricted in ZIF-8 were allowed to escape. The intensity ratio of fluorescence at 469 nm to that at 660 nm (I 469/I 660) was conveniently employed as the response signal for representing the amount of ATP. This nanoprobe exhibits excellent sensitivity and selectivity toward ATP, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.061 μM. Besides, low cytotoxicity of this nanoprobe facilitates its application as a fluorescent indicator in fluorescence imaging of living cells. Encapsulating two types of fluorescent nanomaterials by a degradable ZIF-8 structure makes the ratiometric fluorescence response of the nanocomposite probe towards the target analyte that destroys the ZIF-8 structure possible, and simplifies the application of the probe.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) plays a significant role in life processes of living organisms such as energy cycle, enzyme catalysis and intracellular signal transduction [1,2]. Change of cellular ATP levels in human body was in connection with many pathological processes or diseases, for instance, ischemia [3], inflammation [4], Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's disease [5]. Thus, sensitive and accurate detection of ATP in human fluids or tissues is important to medical research and clinical diagnosis. Various methods including high performance liquid chromatography [6], colorimetry [7], capillary electrophoresis [8], electrochemical [9] and fluorometry [10,11] have been developed for the determination of ATP in organisms in recent years. Among these methods, fluorometry is more considered because of its simplicity, high sensitivity and selectivity, and low cost [12,13], especially by preparing tunable fluorescent nanomaterials as reporters or references [14–16].

Metal-organic framework (MOF) is a kind of crystalline material, which is usually prepared by utilizing the self-assembly of metal ions and organic molecules through coordination bonds [17]. The crystalline porous nature of MOF materials makes them ideal host matrices for encapsulating diverse guests, such as metal nanoclusters [18] and quantum dots [19] to improve optical, chemical, mechanical or electrical performance of the encapsulated guests. As a typical representative of MOFs, zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) composed of zinc ions (Zn2+) and imidazolates has attracted more attention due to its facile preparation and high porosity. Until very recently, many sensors based on ZIF-8 have been developed for detecting metal ions [20] and organic molecules [21,22].

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), as a type of carbon-based nanomaterial with excellent biocompatibility, high chemical stability and low toxicity, have been widely applied in bioanalysis and clinical diagnosis [23,24]. Recent studies reveal that fluorescent gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) exhibit aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) after being encapsulated into MOFs [25,26], owing to the restriction of inter- or intramolecular motions of the molecules modified on surface of AuNCs and the consequent inhibition of the non-radiative energy transfer on the decay of excited states of AuNCs [26].

Herein, we proposed a novel strategy (Scheme 1 ) for sensitive detecting ATP in body fluids by preparing and then employing a nanocomposite CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 as the ratiometric fluorescent probe. With encapsulated CDs and AuNCs in ZIF-8 structure, CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 displays two emission bands at 469 nm and 660 nm under excitation of 360 nm, arising separately from the fluorescent CDs and the AuNCs with AIEE effect. ATP can selectively degrade the ZIF-8 structure by the competative coordination towards Zn2+ with 2-Methylimidazole, resulting in the release of CDs and AuNCs from the nanocomposite. Along with dispersion of the released CDs and AuNCs, fluorescence emission from CDs at 469 nm would increase owing to the efficiency improvement of fluorescence excitation for CDs, while the fluorescence emission from AuNCs would decrease obviously as a result of the inhibition of AIEE effect induced by the degradation of aggregation state of AuNCs. Therefore, the amount of ATP introduced into the sensing system can be indicated sensitively by using the intensity ratio of fluorescence emissions at 469 nm to that at 660 nm (I 469/I 660). The applicability of this strategy was further examined by quantifying ATP in human fluid samples and imaging the distribution of ATP in live cells.

Procedures of preparation of CDs and AuNCs were available in the Supplementary Material file. The as-prepared CDs (100 μL) and AuNCs (1.6 mL) were mixed in aqueous solution of 2-methylimidazole (1.5 M, 3.6 mL), followed by stirring at room temperature for 30 min. Then, aqueous solution of Zn(Ac)2·2H2O (25 mM, 200 μL) was added. The mixture was stirred for 5 h at room temperature to allow coordination of Zn2+ with carboxyl groups and nitrogen atoms of 2-methylimidazole for constructing ZIF-8 structure encapsulating CDs and AuNCs. The product was collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, followed by washing with absolute ethanol for three times. Finally, the product was dispersed in ultrapure water (10 mL) and stored at 4 °C in dark. Similar conditions of reaction and treatment were applied during the preparations of ZIF-8, AuNCs@ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 materials.

CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 (50 μL) and ATP standard solution (100 μL) at various concentration levels were added in Tris-HCl buffer solution (850 μL, 10 mM, pH = 7.4). Then, the mixture was kept at 37 °C for 5 min followed by testing with a fluorophotometer at excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Fluorescence intensity ratio (I 469/I 660) was calculated as the response signal.

Procedures of MTT colorimetric assays for evaluating cellular cytotoxicity of CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8, and fluorescence imaging and colocalization analyses in living cells were available in the Supplementary Material file.

Effects of testing conditions including incubation time and fluorescence excitation wavelength were optimized to improve the analytical performance of the proposed nanoprobe. Results were illustrated in Fig. S4. The incubation time prior to analysis was set at 5 min since it is sufficient for the interaction of ATP and ZIF-8 (Fig. S4A). The excitation wavelength for fluorescence measurement was chosen as 360 nm (Fig. S4B) to get sensitive fluorescence response. The normal pH value (7.4) in human fluid was set as the environmental pH level for sensing ATP.

Testing results illustrated in Fig. 4 A indicate that encapsulating AuNCs into ZIF-8 enhances the fluorescence of AuNCs greatly. Since the fluorescence of AuNCs was influenced by both the state of chemical bonding on particle surface and the aggregation degree of particles [30], XPS measurements (Fig. 2) were conducted to identify the surface bonding states of AuNCs diluted in water and encapsulated in ZIF-8. As indicated in Fig. 2B and D, binding energies of Au(I) and Au (0) components in AuNCs were 84.8 eV and 83.9 eV, respectively. Results of further abundance calculation based on these XPS spectra revealed the amount fractions of Au(I) in total Au element of AuNCs and CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 were 27.59% and 26.30%, respectively, indicating no obvious change of Au bonding state on AuNCs surface after the encapsulation of AuNCs with ZIF-8. Therefore, it is the aggregation of AuNCs induced by the confinement effect of ZIF-8 rather than the change of chemical bonding state on AuNCs surface that contributes to the above improvement of fluorescence emission, which is also known as the aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) effect [25,26].

As can be observed in Fig. 4A and B, fluorescence emission arising from CDs endows the fluorescence of CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 at 469 nm. Moreover, fluorescence at 469 nm enhanced obviously while that at 660 nm varied in an opposite trend after ATP was introduced into the testing system. Because both ATP and 2-Methylimidazole can coordinate with Zn2+, the coordination competition would result in the degradation of ZIF-8 structure in CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 and the consequent release of both AuNCs and CDs. Compared with the restricted CDs in ZIF-8 structure, the released CDs can disperse in the whole test system, which improves the efficiency of their fluorescence excitation remarkably. On the contrary, releasing AuNCs from ZIF-8 structure in CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 reduces the aggregation of AuNCs significantly, leading to the inhibition of AIEE effect.

Fig. 4C and D illustrate the fluorescence decay curves of ZIF-8, CDs, AuNCs and their derivative nanocomposites. The fluorescence lifetime values of dispersed CDs and CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 at 469 nm were 15.60 ns and 13.68 ns, respectively. After adding ATP into the testing system, the lifetime of fluorescence emission at 469 nm (14.33 ns) varied very slightly, indicating the negligible contribution of dynamic quenching approaches during the fluorescence decay processes of CDs. However, the fluorescence lifetime value of CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 at 660 nm was 12.69 μs, while in presence of ATP, this value varied to 8.28 μs and becomes very closed to that of the dispersed AuNCs (7.80 μs). These results confirmed that the dynamic quenching mechanism, which is induced by the inter- and intramolecular motions of the molecules modified on surface of AuNCs, is dominant for the quenching of fluorescence emission of AuNCs after the degradation of ZIF-8 structure in CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8. Fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) of CDs (469 nm) and AuNCs (660 nm) in CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 were 8.23% and 2.32%, respectively. After the reaction of ATP and the nanoprobe, the above QYs changed to be 11.15% and 0.92%.

The resonance light scattering (RLS) spectroscopy of CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 in presence and absence of ATP were demonstrated in Fig. S5. Normally, the aggregation of nanomaterial will lead to enhanced RLS signals [31]. Along with increasing concentration of ATP, RLS signal intensities of the testing system decreased gradually, indicating the decomposition of CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8.

Influence of common coexisting biological substances and ions in human fluids or tissues at their natural concentration levels or 5-fold level of ATP concentration on the detection of ATP were investigated to evaluate the selectivity of the proposed nanoprobe. Results reveal that the above ions or substances have little interference effects during detection processes (Fig. S6), indicating the good selectivity of this nanoprobe.

Fluorescence responses of the proposed nanoprobe towards ATP were demonstrated in Fig. 5 . Along with the concentration increase of ATP, the fluorescence intensity at 469 nm increased gradually while that at 660 nm varied in an opposite trend (Fig. 5A). A linear relationship between the above fluorescence intensity ratios (I 469/I 660) and the ATP concentrations of 0.2 μM–100.0 μM (Fig. 5B) was obtained. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.061 μM (S/N = 3), indicating the comparable or slightly better sensitivity of this nanoprobe for ATP detection than reported methods (Table S1). Additionally, by encapsulating both fluorescent CDs and fluorescent AuNCs into the degradable ZIF-8 structure, the deconstruction of ZIF-8 structure induced by ATP can be evaluated easily by monitoring the fluorescence emission variations of the above fluorescent nanomaterials, that not only facilitates the improvement of response sensitivity of the nanoprobe towards ATP by ratiometric fluorometry, but also simplifies the application of the probe .

Real human fluid samples spiked with ATP were examined for evaluating the practicability of the proposed nanoprobe. Before testing, all the spiked human serum and urine samples were diluted 10-fold and 20-fold, respectively. Results show the recoveries for detecting ATP range from 97.3% to 102.1% (Table 1 ). And, Pearson correlation coefficients (r) values higher than 0.98 were obtained between the results obtained by the present nanoprobe and a commercial ELISA kit (Fig. S7), demonstrating the broad potential application scope of this nanoprobe for detecting ATP in biosamples.

The cellular cytotoxicity of the proposed nanoprobe is dominant for its practical applications. Results of MTT colorimetric assays (Fig. S8A) show the very slight influence of CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 on the viability of HepG2 cells (less than 20%), indicating the low cell toxicity of CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 during cellular ATP detection process.

Encouraged by its excellent biocompatibility, CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 was further applied in fluorescence imaging of intracellular ATP distribution and lysosome localization. As is well known, Ca2+ can enhance intracellular ATP production while lower environmental temperature can inhibit cellular ATP expression. Three groups of HepG2 cells treated with 50 μL of CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 were incubated separately at diverse temperature in presence of 10 mM Ca2+, followed by testing with a confocal microscope (Fig. 6 and S8B). Results reveal the good cell membrane permeability of CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8, which facilitates the fluorescence analysis of ATP in living cells. Moreover, it can be observed clearly that the fluorescence intensities obtained from blue channels varied in an opposite trend to these obtained from red channels (Fig. S8B), which confirmed that ratiometric fluorometry is suitable for quantifying ATP by the present nanoprobe.

The subcellular distribution of CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 was assessed by co-localization experiments. HepG2 cells treated with CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 and LysoTracker Green (a commercial lysosome-specific fluorescent dye) were observed by using a confocal microscope. As shown in Fig. S9, images obtained from blue, red and green channels overlapped quite well. In addition, according to the intensity correlation diagram Fig. S10, the Pearsonʹs colocation coefficients between green channel and other channels were calculated as 0.813 (blue) and 0.808 (red), and the corresponding overlap coefficients were 0.948 (blue) and 0.946 (red), respectively, indicating the lysosome-targeting ability of CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 is comparable to that of LysoTracker Green.

We proposed a dual-emission nanoprobe CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 for highly sensitive determination of ATP by ratiometric fluorometry. Due to the selective decomposition of ZIF-8 structure induced by the coordination competition of ATP and 2-Methylimidazole towards Zn2+, CDs and AuNCs originally encapsulated in the ZIF-8 structure can be released and dispersed, resulting in the improved fluorescence excitation efficiency of CDs and the weakened AIEE effect of AuNCs. Therefore, ratiometric fluorometry was realized for sensitive detection of ATP by employing the intensity ratio of fluorescence arising from CDs (469 nm) to that arising from AuNCs (660 nm) as the response signal, that reduces the distractions of spectral background and the fluctuations of instruments effectively. The present nanoprobe exhibits excellent sensitivity and selectivity for detecting ATP in human fluid samples and imaging intracellular ATP distribution, and demonstrates its promising potential for future applications in bioanalysis and clinical diagnosis.

Wei Zhang: Methodology, Writing - original draft, Data curation, Investigation. Xue Jiang: Writing - original draft, Formal analysis. Yanhua Wu: Writing - original draft, Investigation. Jing Jiang: Methodology, Project administration, Writing - review & editing. Xin Liu: Project administration, Writing - review & editing. Yibing Liu: Project administration, Writing - review & editing. Wei Wang: Project administration, Writing - review & editing. Jinyu Lai: Project administration, Writing - review & editing. Xinghua Wang: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China, the Research Project of Education Department of Jilin Province (No. JJKH20200944KJ) , and the Training Program of Excellent Young Teachers of Jilin University.
